lesson.title: Python errors: meet the traceback
masteryOutcomes: Understand why Python provides a traceback and how it helps identify what went wrong.
Identify parts of a traceback and different types of tracebacks.

concept: Reading Python tracebacks: why they exist, their three parts, the bottom-up reading order, and telling NameError, TypeError, ValueError, and ZeroDivisionError apart
prerequisites: Working with text and numbers: why "5" + 5 fails
Checking the facts: ValueError from impossible conversions like int("hello")

explanations.text: When a program breaks, Python doesn't just shrug — it writes you a little incident report called a traceback. It looks scary: red text, several lines, official-sounding words. But it's actually a map with two treasures on it: WHERE the program broke (the file and line number) and WHAT went wrong (the very last line). The trick nobody tells beginners: read the LAST line first. That's the headline of the whole report.

A traceback has three parts. The header — Traceback (most recent call last): — introduces the report. The location lines — File "main.py", line 2, in <module> plus a copy of the offending source line — say where it happened. The final line gives the error type and message, like NameError: name 'message' is not defined. Read it bottom-up: last line first for what went wrong, then jump to the file and line for where. The type is the diagnosis: NameError means Python met a name it doesn't know (a typo, or a variable used before it's created); TypeError means the values' types don't fit the operation ("5" + 1); ValueError means the operation was fine but the specific value wasn't (int("hello")); ZeroDivisionError means you divided by zero.

Why does the header say 'most recent call last'? A traceback lists the chain of calls that led to the crash, oldest at the top — so the bottom location is where the error actually happened. In a single-file script you see one location, in <module>; once you write functions, the chain grows and bottom-up reading really pays off. Two more distinctions sharpen your reading. First, the reported line is where the error surfaced, not always where the mistake lives — if line 4 crashes adding age + 1, the real bug may be line 1 storing age as text. Second, everything before the crashing line already ran: earlier prints are on screen. The odd one out is SyntaxError — Python catches it before running anything, so nothing executes and there is no call chain, just the file, the line, and a caret pointing near the problem. Newer Python versions (3.11+) even underline the exact failing part of a line with ^ markers.

examples:
print(message)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 1, in <module>
    print(message)
NameError: name 'message' is not defined

age = "21"
print(age + 1)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 2, in <module>
    print(age + 1)
TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str

int("hello")

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 1, in <module>
    int("hello")
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'hello'

print(10 / 0)

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 1, in <module>
    print(10 / 0)
ZeroDivisionError: division by zero

hintLadder.text: Don't read top-down — jump straight to the very last line and say the error type and its message out loud.
Now find the location: which file and which line number does the traceback point at? Go look at that exact line of code.
Match the type to its meaning: NameError — a name Python doesn't know; TypeError — types combined wrongly; ValueError — a value that couldn't be handled; ZeroDivisionError — divided by zero.
Name the fix category — fix a spelling or assignment, convert a type, change the data, or guard the division — and change only that one thing before re-running.

misconceptions.trigger: Treats the whole traceback as unreadable noise and starts changing code at random
misconceptions.correction: A traceback is structured: header, location (file plus line), and a final line with the type and message. Read the last line first, then go to the reported line. One targeted fix beats ten guesses.

misconceptions.trigger: Reads only the header 'Traceback (most recent call last):' and stops
misconceptions.correction: The header carries no diagnosis — it only introduces the report. The answer lives on the LAST line; 'most recent call last' is literally telling you the important end is the bottom.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks NameError means Python is broken or the variable mysteriously vanished
misconceptions.correction: NameError: name 'x' is not defined means Python has never seen an assignment to that exact spelling — almost always a typo or a name used above the line that creates it. Compare the spellings character by character.

TypeError: the kinds of things don't fit the operation — "5" + 1. ValueError: the operation and types were fine, but the specific value wasn't — int("hello"). They call for different fixes: convert or change the operation, versus fix the data.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes the traceback's line number is always where the fix belongs
misconceptions.correction: The line number is where the error surfaced; the cause can live earlier. If line 4 crashes on age + 1, the bug may be line 1 storing age as "21". Start at the reported line, then trace the variable upward.

misconceptions.trigger: Confidently claims an error means the program never ran at all
misconceptions.correction: Everything before the crashing line already executed — earlier prints are on screen to prove it. Python stops at the error line, not at the start. The exception is SyntaxError, which is caught before anything runs.

checks.prompt:
Here's a traceback. Without seeing the program, answer three things: what type of error, on what line, and what's the likely fix?

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 4, in <module>
    print("You are " + age)
TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
acceptanceCriteria: Identifies the error type as TypeError
Locates it at line 4 of main.py
Explains the cause: a string was joined to an int with +
Proposes a valid fix: str(age), a comma in print, or an f-string
checks.passFeedback: That's a professional read: type, place, cause, fix — straight from the report, no guessing.
checks.failFeedback: Use the reading order: last line names TypeError and says a str met an int around +; the File line says line 4. Now, which side of the + needs converting?

checks.prompt: Match each broken line to the error type it raises, with a one-line reason: (a) print(total) where total was never created, (b) int("12.5"), (c) print("5" + 5)
acceptanceCriteria: Matches (a) to NameError because the name was never defined
Matches (b) to ValueError because int() got a value it can't parse
Matches (c) to TypeError because a str and an int don't fit +
checks.passFeedback: You can now tell the three most common tracebacks apart at a glance — that's real diagnostic skill.
checks.failFeedback: Sort by question: is the NAME unknown (NameError), do the TYPES not fit the operation (TypeError), or was the specific VALUE impossible to handle (ValueError)?

practice.prompt: This program crashes. Read the traceback in your head first — name the error type and line — then fix it so it prints exactly: Price each: 4.5
practice.starterCode:
total = 9
items = 2
print("Price each: " + total / items)
acceptanceCriteria: Identifies the crash as a TypeError on the print line (a str joined to a float with +)
Fixes it with a comma in print, str(), or an f-string

practice.prompt: In your own words: why is a traceback a gift rather than a punishment? Name its three parts and say which one you read first.
rubric: It pinpoints where and what went wrong instead of failing silently, turning a mystery into a targeted fix
Parts: the header line, the location lines (file, line number, offending source), and the final type-plus-message line
Read the final line first, then jump to the file and line number

practice.prompt: A program stops with this last line: ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '3.99'. What happened?
options: Python can't do math with decimal numbers
int() was given text that isn't a whole number — like a price string
A variable was used before being defined
A number was divided by zero
practice.explanation: A ValueError from int() means the value couldn't be parsed as a whole number — '3.99' is a decimal string. float("3.99") is the right converter here.

pedagogyRules: Ritualize the reading order every single time an error appears: last line first, say the type and message aloud, then jump to the file and line number.
Never let the student change code before naming the error type and its meaning in one sentence — diagnosis before surgery.
If the student asks a jump-ahead question (try/except, raising errors, debuggers), answer in one line — catching errors comes later; first learn to read them — then return to the parts of the report.
If the student says they already know this, show a fresh traceback they haven't seen and ask for type, line, and fix category; a clean read in under a minute earns a jump straight to the check.
If the student is lost, shrink to a one-line program — print(message) with no message defined — run it, and walk the three parts of its tiny traceback slowly, pointing at each.
When the student is wrong but confident about a cause, point back at the literal last line and read it together word by word — the traceback outranks both of you.
If the student wants to skip ahead, allow it after a first-try pass of the traceback-reading check; otherwise decode one more report together first.
If errors trigger shame or frustration, normalize it in one line — professionals read tracebacks all day; an error message is Python cooperating, not judging — then decode the current one together.
If asked why this matters, answer in one line — traceback fluency is the difference between a thirty-second fix and a lost hour, on every program they'll ever write — then continue.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor invents a cause that contradicts the traceback's message
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Quote the actual last line back and diagnose from it alone: the stated type and message override any guess. A TypeError is never fixed by renaming a variable, and a NameError is never fixed by converting types.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor accepts a vague read like 'there's an error on line 4' as a pass
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Full credit requires the type, what the message means, and the location — ask which part of the traceback said each thing, and don't confirm mastery until all three land.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — tutor hands over the fixed line instead of guiding the read
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Walk the ladder: last line, then location, then type meaning, then fix category — one step at a time, with the student proposing the actual code change themselves.
Never name the error type or the fix before the student commits to their own reading of the traceback — ask what the last line says and wait.

chunks.text: A traceback is Python's incident report when a program crashes: it tells you where it broke and what went wrong, so you can make one targeted fix instead of guessing. Reading order is bottom-up — the last line is the headline. Worked example: print(message) with no message defined produces a report ending in NameError: name 'message' is not defined.

Anatomy of a traceback, top to bottom: the header 'Traceback (most recent call last):' introduces the report; the location lines — File "main.py", line 2, in <module> plus a copy of the offending source line — say where; the final line — the error type, a colon, and the message — says what. 'Most recent call last' means the bottom location is where the error actually happened.

The four common error types: NameError: name 'x' is not defined — a typo or a name used before it's created. TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str — types that don't fit the operation, like "5" + 1. ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'hello' — the operation was fine, the value wasn't, like int("hello"). ZeroDivisionError: division by zero — like 10 / 0. The type is the diagnosis: it tells you which category of fix to reach for.

The line number is where the error surfaced, not always where the mistake lives. Worked example — line 1: age = "21", line 2: print(age + 1). The traceback points at line 2 with a TypeError, but the cause is line 1 storing text. Start at the reported line, then trace the guilty variable upward. Also: everything before the crashing line already ran — earlier prints are on screen.

SyntaxError is the odd one out: Python catches it before the program runs, so nothing executes and there's no call chain — just the file, the line, and a caret pointing near the problem. All the other errors happen mid-run. Newer Python versions (3.11+) also underline the exact failing part of a line with ^ markers inside regular tracebacks — extra precision, same reading order.

celebration.headline: Error messages just became your allies.
celebration.recap: You met the traceback's three parts, learned to read it bottom-up — type and message first, then file and line — and can now tell NameError, TypeError, ValueError, and ZeroDivisionError apart and name the fix category for each.
celebration.unlocked: Next: functions — and with them, tracebacks that show a whole chain of calls, which you now know exactly how to read.
